title: Work & School
shortSummary: Talk about your job, your studies, and your daily working life in Portuguese.
culturalNote: Asking o que você faz? (what do you do?) is friendly, everyday small talk in Brazil. Many job titles change with gender, so a male teacher is professor and a female one professora. Brazilians often blend warmth into the workplace, and the cafezinho break is part of office culture. Students talk about a escola (school) and a faculdade or a universidade (university). The word formação refers to your education or training, and people are usually happy to talk about what they studied and what they hope to do.

grammar.rule: Jobs without an article after ser
grammar.explanation: When stating a profession after ser, Portuguese drops the article: sou médico, sou professora. The article appears only when you add a description: sou um bom médico.
grammar.commonMistake: Always inserting um or uma. Plain professions go without: sou advogado.

grammar.explanation: Gostar always needs de, which contracts with the article: gosto do meu trabalho (de + o), gosto da minha escola (de + a). Never drop the de.
grammar.commonMistake: Forgetting to contract: it is gosto do, not gosto de o.

dialogue.setup: Two people chat at a networking event.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.native: Prazer, Diego. O que você faz?
lines.english: Nice to meet you, Diego. What do you do?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.native: Gosto muito. Termino sempre às seis.
lines.english: I like it a lot. I always finish at six.

lines.speaker: Helena
lines.native: Que bom! Eu às vezes trabalho em casa.
lines.english: How nice! I sometimes work from home.

quiz.question: How do you ask 'What do you do?' in Portuguese?
quiz.explanation: O que você faz? is the standard informal question.

quiz.question: How do you say 'I'm a teacher' (male)?
quiz.explanation: Plain professions after ser drop the article.

quiz.question: What does trabalho em casa mean?
Arrange: trabalho / do / gosto / meu
quiz.explanation: Gosto do meu trabalho means 'I like my job'.
